text: The patient is positioned in the prone jackknife position with the buttocks separated using tapes. The surgeon probes the fistula to assess the defect's extent. A curvilinear incision is made over the perineal body, involving the internal opening. Low voltage cautery is used to raise dermal and anodermal flaps, exposing the internal sphincter. The anterior muscle disruption and scar tissue are visually identified.

The surgeon performs partial mobilization of the skin flaps in the lateral aspects, working within the subcutaneous tissue. The disrupted muscle is identified and retracted, facilitating further mobilization while ensuring the muscle is not skeletonized laterally.

The surgeon identifies the connection point between the levators and the sphincter complex. During anterior dissection, the residual internal sphincter muscle remains connected to the anoderm. The external sphincter muscle is further mobilized, facilitating access to the levators. A similar dissection is performed on the contralateral side, demonstrating the mobilization of the external sphincter muscle.
The retracting tapes are released, allowing for a full overlap of the muscle. The surgeon proceeds to close the vaginal opening using interrupted 3-0 microsutures.

The surgeon performs levatorplasty using OPDS interrupted sutures, ensuring the levators are not confused with the vaginal wall. Three or four sutures are placed to approximate the levators. Following this, a formal overlapping sphincteroplasty is executed with four O-Vicro sutures.
After completing the overlapping sphincteroplasty, the surgeon approximates the skin, focusing on the anterior anoderm defect. The wound is left partially open to allow for drainage.
